privacy.title: Privacy
privacy.not_a_crm_heading: A ticketing system, not a CRM
privacy.not_a_crm_html:
This system helps you sell tickets and check people in. It is deliberately not a CRM (a customer-relationship manager). It does not build marketing profiles, follow people around the web, or keep a tidy address book for you to mine later.

If you want to do CRM things — newsletters, sales pipelines, detailed customer histories — use a proper CRM that is built for it, and feed bookings to it using a listing webhook. Keeping that data over there, rather than here, means less personal information for you to look after and protect.
privacy.aggregate_html:
To run the basics well, the system keeps only a little information:

  • Booking details for each ticket (such as a name and contact details), stored encrypted so they cannot be read straight out of the database.
  • Aggregate numbers — totals like how many tickets sold or how much money was taken. These are not about any one person.
  • A privacy-protecting “have we seen this person before?” note, used for things like the unsubscribe link and spotting returning customers — without keeping their email or phone in a readable form.
privacy.hashing_heading: How the “last seen” note protects people
privacy.hashing_html:
That last note is the privacy-friendly clever bit. Instead of saving someone's email address or phone number, the system runs it through a one-way “mixer” (its proper name is an HMAC hash) and keeps only the scrambled result — a fixed jumble of letters and numbers.

The same email always comes out as the same jumble, so the system can recognise a returning customer or honour an unsubscribe. But the mixer only runs one way: you cannot work backwards from the jumble to the original email or phone. And because the mixer is locked with this site's own secret key, nobody else can reproduce it either.

So the “last seen” note lets the system do its job while holding as little real personal data as possible.
privacy.orphans.heading: Tidy up orphaned records
privacy.orphans.intro_html:
Every ticket holder is attached to a listing. If you delete a listing, anyone who was only on that listing is left behind as an orphaned record — booking details with nothing left to attach them to.

They are harmless, but they are also personal data you no longer need, so it is tidier (and kinder to your visitors) to clear them out. “Older than” is counted from when the booking was made.
privacy.orphans.count: {count, plural, =0 {There are no orphaned records right now.} one {There is # orphaned record right now.} other {There are # orphaned records right now.}}
privacy.orphans.auto_hint: When ticked, the system quietly clears these out for you about once a day. Untick it if you would rather only delete them by hand.
privacy.orphans.error_retention: Please choose how old records must be before they are deleted.
privacy.orphans.flash_purged: {count, plural, =0 {No orphaned records were old enough to delete.} one {Deleted # orphaned record.} other {Deleted # orphaned records.}}
privacy.orphans.log_purged: Privacy: purged {count, plural, =0 {0 orphaned attendee records} one {# orphaned attendee record} other {# orphaned attendee records}}
privacy.erase.heading: Delete one person's record
privacy.erase.intro_html:
If someone asks you to delete their data (a “right to be forgotten” request under GDPR), use this to remove the privacy-protecting “last seen” note tied to their email or phone — their unsubscribe state, how often you have contacted them, and their returning-customer history.

This does not delete their individual ticket bookings. Those belong to each listing — open the Attendees page to find and delete a specific booking. Each email and phone number is its own separate note, so if someone gave you both, delete each one.
privacy.erase.identifier_hint: Type it exactly as they gave it to you when booking.
privacy.erase.error_identifier: Please enter the email address or phone number to delete.
privacy.erase.error_type: Please choose whether you are entering an email or a phone number.
privacy.erase.flash_none: No record was found for that email or phone, so there was nothing to delete.
privacy.erase.log_done: Privacy: erased a contact record (GDPR request)
